Comparison to Single-modal Methods Due to the lack of existing approaches leveraging mul- timodal data for few-shot learning, we additionally com- pare our approach to existing methods using only im- age data during training. Outperforming these state-of- the-art image-only few-shot learning proves the beneﬁcial impact of additional text data during training. Speciﬁ- cally, we compare our method with MAML [9], meta- learning LSTM [30], matching networks [39], prototypi- cal networks [37] and metric-agnostic conditional embed- dings [13]. The results for CUB-200 for these methods are provided in [4]. We also include theirs results in our com- parison. However, their experimental setup differs slightly from our evaluation protocol. Instead of performing 50-way classiﬁcation, the results in [4] are reported for 5-way clas- siﬁcation in the 1- and 5-shot scenarios. This implies that in every few-shot learning episode, 5 random classes are sampled for which a classiﬁcation task has to be solved, followed by the choice of nsamples that are available per class. For the sake of comparability, we also evaluated our approach in the same experimental setup. We repeat our experiment for 600 episodes and report average top-1 accu- racy and 95% conﬁdence intervals in Tab. 4. It can be observed that even our image-only baseline, which performs nearest neighbor classiﬁcation using pro- totypes in our modiﬁed ResNet-18 feature representation reaches state-of-the-art accuracies. Note that out image- only baseline can be interpreted as ResNet-version of Proto- typical Network [37], which uses a simpler model network architecture in its original version. Including multimodal data during training outperforms the other approaches in both 1- and 5-shot learning scenarios. This proves the strength of our nearest neighbor baseline and shows that enriching the embedding space ϕ with generated features conditioned on data from other modalites further improves the classiﬁcation accuracies. In Fig. 3 we show a tSNE vi- sualization of the embedding space ϕincluding the image- only and multimodal prototypes pI and pM respectively in the 5-way classiﬁcation task. The graph clearly shows some clusters indicating the class membership. It can be observed that the generated feature vectors shift the prototypes into regions where more unseen test samples can be classiﬁed correctly. Moreover, Fig. 3 shows retrieval results of un- seen classes for pI and pM . See further retrieval results in the Supplementary Materials. 5. Analysis In order to get a further in-depth understanding of cer- tain aspects of our method, we performed some additional experiments analyzing its behavior. To this end, we use the CUB-200 dataset for the experiments in this section. 5.1. Reducing Textual Data In a ﬁrst experiment we want to analyze the importance of the amount of available textual descriptions. Note that for the experiments in Tab. 3 we used all 10 textual descrip- tions per image to generate a feature vector Gt(Φ(ti). In this experiment we want to understand how the model be- haves at reduced text availability. Therefore, in addition to limiting the amount of available images per novel class to n, we limit the amount of textual descriptions per image to k ∈{1,2,5,10}. We evaluate the classiﬁcation accuracy for n ∈{1,2,5}with reduced number of textual descrip-0 2 4 6 8 10 1 1.02 1.04 1.06 1.08 1.1 1.12 Amount of Texts per Image Relative Accuracy Gain 1-shot 2-shot 5-shot Figure 4: Relative top-5-accuracy gain for different amounts of available texts. The y-axis shows the accuracy gain in relation to the image-only baseline and the x-axis the amount of available texts per image k. tions. In Fig. 4 we show the relative accuracy gains for the different amount of texts compared to the image-only base- line. The x-axis shows the amount of texts and the y-axis the relative accuracy gain. It can be observed that the lower the amount of images n the higher is the accuracy gain given the text. The graphs show an increasing trend which indi- cates that the more texts are available the more the classi- ﬁcation accuracies can be increased. This proves our as- sumption that enriching the embedding spaceϕis crucial to reach high classiﬁcation results. Interestingly, in every n- shot scenario the second text leads to the highest accuracy gain. However, adding more text constantly improves the results and is never harmful to the model. 5.2. Impact of Prototype Shift We investigate how the adjustment of a certain prototype impacts the classiﬁcation performance. Therefore, we ana- lyze the per-class accuracy gain in correlation with the shift of the prototype when exposed to multimodal data. The assumption we want to conﬁrm whether large adjustments to the prototype go along with higher accuracy gain com- pared to classes for which the prototype remains almost unchanged. To this end, we measure change in prototype between the original image-only prototype pI and the up- dated multimodal prototype pM using the cosine distance denoted by d(pI,pM ). For every novel class, we analyze the correlation of the prototype update to the accuracy gain compared to the image-only baseline. In Fig. 5 we show the per-class accuracy gain for all prototypes in the 1-shot sce- nario. The x-axis shows the rank of the prototypes for all 50 novel classes of the CUB-200 dataset sorted by d(pM ,pI) in a descending order. The y-axis represents the accuracy gain for the certain prototype. We report top-5 accuracy and 0 10 20 30 40 50 0 0.05 0.1 0.15 Prototypes sorted byd(pI,pM) Per-Class Accuracy Gain Figure 5: Per-class accuracy gain for prototypes after the adjustment with generated feature vectors. The x-axis shows the rank of the prototype sorted by d(pI,pM ) and the y-axis the top-5-accuracy gain for that particular class. show the average of the result for 100 few-shot episodes. It can be observed that the more the prototype is changed (low rank) the higher is the accuracy gain for this particular class. On average, the most changed prototype leads to a per-class top-5 accuracy gain of ca. 15%. Smaller changes have a smaller impact on the classiﬁcation performance and on average, adjusting the prototype with multimodal data is not harmful for the accuracy. This suggests that the mul- timodal features carry complimentary information that is used to simulate unseen novel class samples. At the same time it shows that the text-to-image feature mapping is well learned, as the most diverse, or farthest multimodal features net the largest performance gains. 6. Conclusion and Future Work In this paper we tackled the few-shot learning problem from a multimodal perspective. Therefore, we proposed to leverage a nearest neighbor classiﬁer in a powerful rep- resentation space. To mitigate the low population prob- lem caused by the few-shot scenario we developed a cross- modal generation framework that is capable of enriching the visual feature space given data in another modality (e.g. text). Classiﬁcation can now be performed by ﬁnding the nearest multimodal class prototype to an unseen test sam- ple. We evaluated our proposed methods on the two mul- timodal datasets CUB-200 and Oxford-102 and showed the applicability of our approach. We outperformed our strong baselines and state-of-the-art single-modal and multimodal methods by a large margin. For future work we plan to fol- low the notion of [35] which entails optimizing jointly the learned representation on multimodal data.References [1] M. Bauer, M. Rojas-Carulla, J. B. ´Swiatkowski, B. new_method.method:
Title: BEMeGA — Bound‑conditioned Episodic Metric and Geometry Adapter for Robust Few‑shot Prototypes

Motivation
- Why now: Prototype-based few-shot learners generalize poorly when test shots differ from training (k-mismatch), classes are anisotropic or multimodal, or the target domain shifts. Prior fixes are either global and static (L2/LDA/EST) or fully transductive (protoLP/LaplacianShot), and thus cannot tailor the metric, dimensionality, or prototype structure to each episode. Per-class covariance approaches (e.g., FeCAM) help under many-shot but are fragile with tiny k and do not detect when multi-prototype structure is needed.
- Missing piece: Recent bounds for prototype classifiers isolate three controllable determinants of generalization: (i) variance of feature norms, (ii) within/between scatter ratio, and (iii) heterogeneity of class covariance traces. There is no lightweight, support-only mechanism that, per episode, co-adapts (a) intrinsic dimension, (b) metric anisotropy, and (c) prototype multiplicity to target these terms, and only escalates to transduction when it is likely to help.
- Goal (汎化性能の改善): Learn an amortized, per-episode adapter that uses only support statistics to choose a low-complexity geometry—projection, covariance, and number of prototypes—that reduces the bound’s unfavorable terms; invoke a tiny, calibrated transductive refinement only on predicted hard episodes.

Methodology
High-level: Given the support set of an episode, a small adapter H predicts a PSD-by-design geometry and a simple mixture-of-prototypes classifier. H is meta-trained on base classes with query supervision and auxiliary penalties that proxy the bound’s terms. A risk gate decides whether to run 3–5 iterations of transductive refinement; otherwise inference stays inductive and fast.

1) Bound-aware backbone pretraining
- Objective: cross-entropy + penalties that nudge embeddings toward favorable bound terms.
  - Norm-variance penalty: reduce variance of squared feature norms across samples.
  - Spectral margin regularizer: increase between- vs within-class scatter.
  - Trace-equality penalty: reduce dispersion of per-class covariance traces.
- Dictionary bank for stable geometry: store orthonormal bases U_i of the leading eigenspaces of class-mean covariance Σ_μ, mean within-class covariance Σ_s, and Σ_μ − ρ Σ_s across a small ρ grid; plus per-dimension variance atoms. Later, episode projections and low-rank covariances are built as sparse convex combinations of these atoms (ensures PSD, few parameters).

2) Episodic adapter H (support-only)
- Inputs (support statistics): k, N; per-class means and pairwise distances; feature norm moments and their variance; diagonal covariance traces with James–Stein shrinkage when k≤5; multimodality proxies (intra-class 1-NN edge ratio, silhouette with small-sample correction); cross-class trace dispersion; a domain-shift proxy (episode norm-scale vs base mean norm).
- Outputs (per episode):
  a) Projection P and intrinsic dimension d: P = Σ softmax(w)·U_i followed by column gating to select top-d; Stiefel penalty keeps near-orthonormal. A temperature τ_P rescales distances after projection.
  b) Per-class covariances Σ_y (diagonal+low-rank): Σ_y = σ^2 I + B B^T with rank r≤d. Columns of B come from the dictionary; meta-learned shrinkages (α1 for I, α2 for off-diagonals), and a shared↔class-specific blend to suppress trace heterogeneity under tiny k; optional correlation normalization flag. Woodbury inversion for speed and stability.
  c) Prototype multiplicity m_y∈{1,2,3}: sparsity prior discourages over-fragmentation. Initialize with spherical k-means in P-space (≤5 iters). For 1-shot, seed centers via farthest-point on a tiny label-preserving augmentation bank (no test-time weight updates).
  d) Risk predictor Ř and refinement knob λ: a small MLP over bound proxies (norm-variance, within/between ratio, trace dispersion, d/r penalties, multimodality score) predicts Ř. If Ř>θ (threshold via split-conformal on base validation episodes), run 3–5 fast iterations of a transductive refinement (protoLP or LaplacianShot) in P-space with predicted λ; else remain inductive.
- Decision rule (inductive default): ŷ = argmin_y min_j (x−μ_{y,j})^T Σ_y^{-1} (x−μ_{y,j}) − log π_{y,j}.

3) Training and safeguards
- Meta-objective per episode: query cross-entropy through the Mahalanobis mixture head + bound-proxy regularization (penalize norm-variance, poor within/between ratio, inter-class trace dispersion) + complexity penalties that prefer small d and r when k is small. Regularize τ_P to avoid extreme scaling.
- Stability by construction: PSD covariances, orthogonality penalty on P, sparsity on m_y, gradient stops on discrete gates, Woodbury inverses with jitter.
- Risk calibration: train Ř to predict held-out query loss; fit an isotonic link for monotonicity in proxies. Choose θ via split-conformal calibration to cap the transductive call-rate and guarantee non-negative expected improvement under shift.

4) Practical integration (drop-in for ProtoNets/SimpleShot)
- Minimal code additions: an adapter H that consumes support stats and emits weights over dictionary atoms, d, r, shrinkages α1/α2, shared↔specific blend, correlation-normalization flag, m_y and priors π_y, τ_P, and (Ř, λ).
- Utilities: projection builder with re-orthonormalization; diagonal+low-rank covariance factory with shrinkage and Woodbury inverse; spherical k-means; optional transductive branch.
- Complexity: O(ND) to collect stats; O(Dd + dr^2 + Nd m̄) to build P, Σ_y, and prototypes; transductive gate preserves inductive speed in most episodes.

What is new vs. prior work
- vs L2/LDA/EST and EST-ProtoNet: those are global post-hoc linear transforms with fixed dimension/metric; they cannot adapt to episode k, anisotropy, or multimodality. BEMeGA learns a support-conditioned projection P, intrinsic dimension d, per-class anisotropy Σ_y, and m_y per episode, explicitly targeting the recent prototype bounds.
- vs FeCAM: FeCAM fits per-class covariances with fixed feature space and no episode-level dimension control; it applies uniform shrinkage and no multi-prototype selection. BEMeGA meta-learns shrinkage strengths, rank r, correlation normalization and a shared↔specific blend; it also chooses d and m_y per episode, avoiding unstable full-covariance estimation at tiny k.
- vs protoLP/LaplacianShot: Transductive solvers are not used by default and can hurt easy episodes. BEMeGA is inductive-first and only triggers a tiny transductive refinement when a calibrated risk gate predicts a gain.
- vs topology/augmentation methods (TDD, Meta-MaxUp, LP-A3): Orthogonal and complementary. BEMeGA controls the per-episode geometry and classifier complexity from support statistics rather than enforcing topology or searching worst-case augmentations.

Evaluation plan (robustness-first)
- Datasets/backbones: miniImageNet, tieredImageNet, CIFAR-FS, FC100; cross-domain miniImageNet→CUB; ResNet12/18 and ViT.
- Protocol: Sweep test k in 1…10 and N in 2…20; report k-mismatch curves; domain-shift performance.
- Baselines: ProtoNet, SimpleShot (L2/CL2N), LDA/EST, FeCAM (full/diag with shrinkage), protoLP/LaplacianShot (unconditional), multi-prototype baselines.
- Metrics: mean accuracy±95% CI; transductive call-rate and win-rate; calibration metrics for Ř; runtime. Ablations: P only, Σ only, multi-prototypes only, gate only; dictionary size; shot-aware penalties.

Expected impact
- Accuracy and robustness: Consistent gains across varying shots/ways and under domain shift via episode-tailored choices of intrinsic dimension, anisotropy, and prototype multiplicity. Substantially reduced k-mismatch sensitivity.
- Efficiency and practicality: Inductive-first inference with a few linear-algebra ops and ≤5 clustering steps; the transductive branch is rare and calibrated, keeping latency near ProtoNet/SimpleShot.
- Interpretability and control: Per-episode diagnostics (chosen d, r, m_y, Ř) expose actionable knobs (e.g., when to enable transduction or request more labels), useful for active/continual few-shot.
- Breadth: Drop-in for prototype heads in detection/segmentation and retrieval; applicable to exemplar-free continual learning via low-rank covariance updates; provides a principled, bound-driven way to trade dimension/complexity for stability on a per-task basis.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
BEMeGA — Experimental Plans (3)

Common setup across experiments
- Code stack: PyTorch (>=2.1), NumPy, SciPy, faiss (optional for k-means), scikit-learn (metrics, isotonic regression), torchmetrics.
- Backbones: ResNet-12 or ResNet-18 for CNN; optionally a ViT-S/16. Train once per dataset with the bound-aware pretraining losses, then freeze. Experiments primarily operate in feature space to keep runtime modest.
- Bound-aware pretraining (single pass per dataset):
  - Optimizer: SGD+Nesterov (0.9), lr 0.1 cosine, wd 5e-4, 200–240 epochs.
  - Loss: CE + λ_nv Var(||f(x)||^2) + λ_fr trace(S_w)/trace(S_b) + λ_te Std_y[trace(Σ_y)], with λ_nv=0.1, λ_fr=0.1, λ_te=0.05 (tune lightly on base-val).
  - Dictionary bank building (once): compute eigenspaces U_i of Σ_μ, Σ_s, and Σ_μ−ρΣ_s for ρ∈{0.1,0.3,0.5}; store top-64 vectors per atom; store per-dimension variance atoms (diagonal) by bucketing dims by variance quantiles (K=8 bins). Save to disk.
- Adapter H meta-training (episodic):
  - Episodes: sample N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}, q=15/query per class from base classes.
  - Inputs: support stats: per-class mean, pairwise distances, squared-norm moments and variance, James–Stein-shrunk diagonal cov traces, intra-class 1-NN ratio, silhouette with small-sample correction, cross-class trace dispersion, norm-scale vs base mean, etc.
  - Outputs: P (weights over dictionary atoms + column gating for d), τ_P, per-class Σ_y via diag+low-rank (rank r), shrinkages α1/α2, shared↔class-specific blend β, correlation-normalization flag, m_y ∈ {1,2,3} and priors, Ř and λ for optional transductive refinement.
  - Loss: query CE through mixture-of-prototypes + bound-proxy regularizers (same 3 as above, measured on support) + complexity penalties c_d·d + c_r·r with c_d,c_r scaled up when k≤3; τ_P L2 penalty; sparsity penalty on m_y.
  - Optimizer: AdamW lr 1e-3, wd 1e-4, 60k–100k episodes, batch size 4 episodes.
  - Safeguards: PSD-by-design, Woodbury inverses with 1e-4 jitter, Stiefel penalty on P.
- Inference core (inductive default): ŷ = argmin_y min_j (x−μ_{y,j})^T Σ_y^{-1} (x−μ_{y,j}) − log π_{y,j}, in P-space with distance scale τ_P.
- Transductive refinement (optional): LaplacianShot or protoLP for 3–5 iterations in P-space using λ from H. Implement both; choose via validation.
- Statistical protocol: 10,000 episodes per condition unless noted; report mean ± 95% CI via bootstrap over episodes; paired tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank) vs main baselines per episode alignment.

Experiment 1 — k-mismatch robustness on in-domain few-shot
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Show BEMeGA reduces sensitivity to the number of shots at test time and improves accuracy across ways/shots compared to static geometry and unconditional transduction.
- Hypotheses: (H1) Higher accuracy across k∈{1,2,5,8,10}, N∈{5,10,15,20}. (H2) Flatter k-mismatch curve (smaller accuracy drop away from meta-train k). (H3) Comparable latency to inductive baselines due to low transductive call-rate.

Data/backbones
- miniImageNet and tieredImageNet. Use canonical base/novel splits. Backbone: ResNet-12.

Baselines
- ProtoNet (L2), SimpleShot (CL2N), LDA/EST transforms, FeCAM (diag and full with shrinkage), LaplacianShot and protoLP (unconditional), Multi-proto ProtoNet (fixed m=2), and ablations of BEMeGA: P-only, Σ-only, m-only, no-gate (always inductive), unconditional transductive, shared-only covariances.

Protocol
- Meta-train H on base classes with N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5} mixture episodes. Freeze backbones and dictionary.
- Evaluate on novel classes with grid: N∈{5,10,15,20}, k∈{1,2,5,8,10}. q=15/query per class.
- For each condition, sample 10k episodes. Record adapter decisions (d, r, m_y, τ_P, Ř) per episode.

Metrics
- Accuracy ±95% CI. k-mismatch sensitivity: slope of accuracy vs |k−k_train| (fit linear model per method), area-under-k-curve. Runtime per episode (ms), transductive call-rate and win-rate.

Analyses
- Accuracy tables against baselines. Plot accuracy vs k for each N. Report slope and relative reduction vs ProtoNet/FeCAM.
- Scatter: chosen d and r vs k; expect smaller d,r for small k, larger when k grows.
- Correlate bound proxies (support-measured) with Ř and accuracy gains.

Success criteria
- Statistically significant gains over L2/EST/FeCAM at k∈{1,2} and sustained gains at k≥5. 20–40% reduction in mismatch slope vs ProtoNet. Call-rate ≤25% with >60% win-rate when called.

Minimal PyTorch-style sketch (core inference)
- Build support stats S.
- P, Σ_y, m_y, τ_P, (Ř,λ) = H(S).
- If Ř>θ: run 3–5 iters LaplacianShot in P-space; else inductive.
Pseudo-code:
  feats = backbone(images).detach()
  S = compute_support_stats(feats_support, labels_support)
  P, params = adapter(S)  # includes d, r, Σ_y, m_y, τ_P, Ř, λ
  Zs = (feats_support @ P) * params.tau_P; Zq = (feats_query @ P) * params.tau_P
  protos = init_multi_protos(Zs, labels_support, m=params.m)
  covs = build_diag_lowrank_covs(Zs, labels_support, params)
  if params.risk_hat > theta:
      logits = laplacian_shot(Zs, labels_support, Zq, protos, covs, params.lambda)
  else:
      logits = mahalanobis_mixture_logits(Zq, protos, covs, params.pi)
  pred = logits.argmax(-1)

Experiment 2 — Stress-test anisotropy and multimodality (controlled + real)
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Validate that per-class anisotropic covariances and multi-prototype selection drive gains when classes are anisotropic/multimodal.
- Hypotheses: (H4) On synthetic Gaussian episodes with known structure, BEMeGA selects correct m_y and improves accuracy over spherical metrics. (H5) On real images with induced multimodality, BEMeGA’s m>1 and low-rank covariances yield significant gains.

A) Controlled synthetic episodes (Python-only, fast)
- Data generator: R^D with D=50. Sample N classes/episode. For each class y:
  - Choose mode count M∈{1,2,3} with P(M=1)=0.6, P(2)=0.3, P(3)=0.1.
  - For each mode j: draw center μ_{y,j} ~ N(0, σ_c^2 I), σ_c=3.
  - Draw anisotropic covariance: Σ_{y,j} = Q diag(λ) Q^T with Q~Haar on O(D), λ_i ∝ i^{-p}, p~Uniform[0.5,2.0], scaled to target trace τ~Uniform[0.5,2.0].
  - Training episodes: N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}, q=20. 100k episodes for training H from scratch (you can bypass image backbone; adapter learns directly on synthetic stats; dictionary atoms sampled from random orthonormal sets and diag bins).
- Baselines: spherical ProtoNet (Euclidean), global LDA projection + spherical, diagonal Mahalanobis with shared shrinkage, multi-proto with fixed m=2 (spherical), FeCAM-style diagonal covariance with uniform shrinkage.
- Metrics: accuracy ±95% CI, m_y selection accuracy (compare predicted m_y vs true M>1 indicator), selected rank r vs ground-truth spectrum decay (correlation), chosen d vs optimal PCA dimension (oracle from explained variance threshold 90%).
- Expectation: BEMeGA ≫ spherical on multimodal/anisotropic episodes; near-tied on spherical unimodal ones while choosing m=1 and small r.

B) Real multimodality: Dual-mode CIFAR-FS
- Construct dataset: CIFAR-FS base/novel splits. For each image at training and testing, with prob 0.5 apply a 90° rotation or strong color-jitter+solarize; keep original label. The two appearance modes create multimodality per class.
- Backbone: ResNet-12 trained bound-aware on augmented base.
- Protocol: Meta-train H on base; evaluate on novel classes with N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,2,5}. 10k episodes/condition.
- Baselines: same as Experiment 1.
- Metrics: accuracy, proportion of episodes where BEMeGA chooses m>1, and accuracy uplift conditioned on m>1 vs m=1. Also evaluate a forced single-prototype variant of BEMeGA to isolate m.
- Expectation: Significant gains when induced multimodality is strong (rotation+color-jitter); BEMeGA auto-selects m>1 in >60% of episodes and yields >3–5% absolute accuracy gain over spherical baselines at k∈{1,2}.

Implementation notes
- Reuse the same adapter codepath; for synthetic, disable transductive gate to isolate inductive geometry effects.
- For m selection, initialize via spherical k-means (≤5 iters) in P-space; sparsity penalty to avoid over-fragmentation.

Experiment 3 — Cross-domain robustness and calibrated transductive gating
Goal and hypothesis
- Goal: Demonstrate inductive-first efficiency and calibrated transductive refinement that triggers rarely yet improves accuracy under shift.
- Hypotheses: (H6) On miniImageNet→CUB, BEMeGA-inductive already outperforms static baselines; (H7) Gate triggers on hard episodes with positive win-rate and bounded call-rate; (H8) Split-conformal θ ensures non-negative expected improvement and better runtime-accuracy trade-off than unconditional transduction.

Data/backbones
- Train backbone and H on miniImageNet base; evaluate on miniImageNet novel (in-domain) and CUB (cross-domain) novel. Backbone: ResNet-12 and ViT-S/16.

Gating setup
- Risk predictor Ř trained to predict held-out query CE on base-val episodes (support-only proxies). Fit isotonic regression g(Ř) for calibration.
- Split-conformal on a held-out calibration set (e.g., 5k episodes from base-val and 5k from a small portion of target domain if allowed/unlabeled) to choose θ such that P(g(Ř)>θ | improvement<0) ≤ α (e.g., α=0.1). Implement by computing per-episode improvement of 3-iter LaplacianShot over inductive on features from a proxy domain; set θ at the α-quantile of g(Ř) on negatively improving episodes.

Protocol
- Evaluate on novel miniImageNet and novel CUB with N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}. 10k episodes/condition.
- Methods: BEMeGA-inductive only; BEMeGA+Gate (calibrated); Unconditional LaplacianShot/protoLP in P-space; ProtoNet/FeCAM/LDA as in Exp 1.

Metrics
- Accuracy ±95% CI; call-rate (% episodes where gate fires); win-rate (P[transduction improves accuracy]); expected improvement (mean accuracy delta gated vs inductive-only); runtime per episode; calibration metrics for g(Ř): ECE, Brier score, reliability plots.

Analyses
- Report accuracy, call-rate, win-rate, expected improvement. Compare runtime-accuracy Pareto vs unconditional transduction.
- Plot accuracy vs call-rate by sweeping θ. Highlight operating point from conformal calibration.

Success criteria
- BEMeGA+Gate achieves higher accuracy than inductive-only, within 10–20% of unconditional transduction’s gain but at ≤30% of its runtime cost; expected improvement ≥ 0 by construction; ECE < 0.1.

Key ablations to run once and reuse conclusions
- Remove bound-aware pretraining penalties; observe drops in both bound proxies and accuracy.
- Disable dictionary (use free linear P); check stability and PSD issues; expect worse calibration and higher variance.
- Force d=full or r=0; quantify effect on tiny-k.
- Force single prototype m=1; quantify effect on multimodality stress tests.

Logging and diagnostics (all experiments)
- Per-episode: d, r, m_y, τ_P, Σ trace dispersion before/after adaptation, within/between scatter ratio, norm variance, Ř, whether gate fired, improvement from transduction when run.
- Aggregate: histograms of d,r,m_y vs k; correlation of bound proxies with accuracy; feature norm variance distributions.

Reproducibility details
- Seeds: 5 seeds; report mean across seeds and CI across episodes nested within seeds.
- Hardware: 1×A100 or 3090; experiments run with frozen features after pretraining; meta-training H ~6–8 GPU hours per dataset.
- Episode counts: 10k per condition; synthetic: 50k for precise curves.

Minimal helper implementations (sketches)
- Support stats:
  def support_stats(Z, y):
      per_class = [Z[y==c] for c in unique(y)]
      means = [X.mean(0) for X in per_class]
      traces = [X.var(0, unbiased=True).sum() for X in per_class]
      js_traces = james_stein_traces(per_class)  # shrink small-k
      nn_intra = intra_class_nn_ratio(Z, y)
      sil = small_sample_silhouette(Z, y)
      norm_var = (Z.pow(2).sum(dim=1)).var(unbiased=False)
      scatter = within_between_traces(Z, y)  # returns (tw, tb)
      return {…}
- Projection from atoms:
  def build_P(weights, atoms, d):
      U = sum(w*a for w,a in zip(weights, atoms))  # concatenate then re-orthonormalize via QR
      Q,_ = torch.linalg.qr(U)
      return Q[:, :d]
- Diag+low-rank covariance and Woodbury inverse:
  def lowrank_cov_inv(B, sigma2):
      # Σ = sigma2 I + B B^T
      inv = lambda X: X / sigma2 - (X @ B) @ torch.linalg.inv(torch.eye(B.size(1))+ (B.T @ X @ B)/sigma2) @ (B.T / sigma2)
      return inv
- Spherical k-means init for m_y:
  def spherical_kmeans(X, m, iters=5):
      C = init_farthest_points(X, m)
      for _ in range(iters):
          assign = cosine_sim(X, C).argmax(1)
          C = torch.stack([F.normalize(X[assign==j].mean(0),dim=0) for j in range(m)])
      return C

What each experiment uniquely demonstrates
- Exp1: Episode-tailored geometry reduces k-mismatch and improves across-way/shot accuracy while staying efficient.
- Exp2: The anisotropic metric and multi-prototype mechanism are necessary and effective under class shape/multimodality shifts; adapter selects appropriate complexity (d,r,m).
- Exp3: The calibrated risk gate safely leverages transduction only when it helps, preserving latency and improving robustness under domain shift.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
BEMeGA — Detailed Experimental Specification and Implementation Plan

This document expands each item in the Experiment Strategy with concrete implementation details, reliability safeguards, and PyTorch-oriented code sketches using existing libraries wherever possible. It is organized as follows:
- Common setup across experiments (data, backbones, pretraining, dictionary, adapter H meta-training, inference, gating/calibration, statistics)
- Experiment 1: k-mismatch robustness on in-domain few-shot
- Experiment 2: Stress-test anisotropy and multimodality (A: synthetic, B: real)
- Experiment 3: Cross-domain robustness and calibrated transductive gating
- Key ablations (run once; reuse across experiments)
- Logging and diagnostics
- Reproducibility details
- Minimal helper implementations (PyTorch-oriented sketches)


Common setup across experiments

Code stack and environment
- Python >= 3.10
- PyTorch >= 2.1 with CUDA; optional torch.compile for speed; torchmetrics for classification metrics
- NumPy, SciPy (stats, sparse ops), scikit-learn (metrics, KMeans, NearestNeighbors, LabelSpreading, LedoitWolf, IsotonicRegression), faiss-gpu (optional for k-means and kNN graph)
- Plotting: matplotlib or seaborn
- Logging: wandb or tensorboardX; structured JSON logging to disk for per-episode diagnostics
- Determinism: set torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=False and seed per run; allow slight nondeterminism only where speed-critical

Data and backbones
- Datasets and splits: miniImageNet, tieredImageNet, CIFAR-FS, FC100; for cross-domain, miniImageNet→CUB. Use canonical base/val/novel splits.
- Backbones: ResNet-12 (primary), ResNet-18; optionally ViT-S/16 with small patch size (e.g., timm pretrained ImageNet then fine-tuned bound-aware)
- Preprocessing: standard 84×84 (mini/tiered), 32×32 (CIFAR-FS/FC100). RandAugment or standard augmentations for pretraining; center crop for evaluation.
- Feature extraction: after pretraining, freeze features; store features on disk per split to accelerate episodic meta-training and evaluation.

Bound-aware backbone pretraining
- Optimizer: SGD with Nesterov momentum=0.9, weight decay=5e-4, cosine LR schedule from 0.1 to 1e-4 over 200–240 epochs; warmup 5 epochs at 0.01.
- Loss per batch: L = CE + λ_nv Var(||f(x)||^2) + λ_fr trace(S_w)/trace(S_b) + λ_te Std_y[trace(Σ_y)]
  - λ_nv=0.1, λ_fr=0.1, λ_te=0.05; lightly tune on base-val (±0.05)
  - Implementation:
    - Norm-variance: compute squared feature norms across batch; variance with unbiased=False for stability
    - Spectral margin (Fisher ratio proxy): for current batch with labels, compute within-class scatter S_w and between-class scatter S_b using class means; use trace ratio; add small epsilon to denominator
    - Trace-equality penalty: per-class covariance trace (use LedoitWolf diagonalized variance to stabilize, or unbiased var sum); take standard deviation across classes
- Regularization: label smoothing (0.1), mixup/cutmix optional; gradient clipping 1.0; AMP (bf16 or fp16) where supported
- Checkpoints: save best CE on base-val and best combined bound proxy metric (normalized sum of the 3 penalties)

Dictionary bank building (once per dataset and backbone)
- Compute on base training set features:
  - Class means {μ_c}; covariance of means Σ_μ; pooled within-class covariance Σ_s
  - For ρ ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}, compute A_ρ = Σ_μ − ρ Σ_s
  - For each matrix in {Σ_μ, Σ_s, A_ρ}, compute top-64 eigenvectors via torch.linalg.eigh (or scipy.sparse.linalg if D large). Atoms U_i ∈ R^{D×d_i}, typically store each as D×1 or packed D×k blocks; persist as float32
  - Per-dimension variance atoms (diagonal atoms): bucket feature dimensions by variance quantiles (K=8 bins); store 8 one-hot diagonal selectors and their mean variances to construct diagonal shrinkage or low-rank B selection
- Store bank to disk (TorchScript or .pt): list of orthonormal bases and diag atoms; include mean feature norm to normalize episode norm-scale proxy

Adapter H meta-training (episodic)
- Episode sampling (base classes): N ∈ {5,10}, k ∈ {1,5}, q=15 queries per class; batch 4 episodes on GPU
- Support statistics S per episode (support-only):
  - k, N, per-class means μ_y, pairwise distances between μ_y, squared-norm moments E||z||^2 and Var||z||^2, per-class diagonal covariance traces (shrunken when k≤5), intra-class 1-NN edge ratio, silhouette score with small-sample correction, cross-class trace dispersion, norm-scale vs base mean norm; optionally Shapiro–Wilk normality surrogate (skew/kurtosis) of class features
  - Use LedoitWolf() from sklearn for shrinkage when k≥5; James–Stein style shrinkage to pooled trace when k≤5 (see code sketch)
- Adapter outputs per episode:
  - Projection P and dimension d: convex weights over dictionary atoms → sum → QR re-orthonormalization → select first d; learn d via relaxed gate (e.g., sigmoid gates with hard top-k at inference); apply Stiefel penalty ||P^T P−I||_F^2; distance scale τ_P
  - Per-class Σ_y: diagonal+low-rank Σ_y = σ^2 I + B B^T where B’s columns are selected from dictionary atoms projected into P-space; rank r ≤ d; shrinkages α1 (I) and α2 (off-diagonal) and shared↔class-specific blend β (β=0 shared, β=1 class-specific) to reduce trace heterogeneity when tiny k; optional correlation normalization flag toggles normalization of features by per-dimension std
  - Prototype multiplicity m_y ∈ {1,2,3} with sparsity prior; initialize centers via spherical k-means (≤5 iters) in P-space; for 1-shot, farthest-point on tiny augmentation bank (e.g., color jitter, flip) without updating network weights
  - Risk predictor Ř and transduction knob λ (regularization for LaplacianShot/protoLP)
- Loss per episode:
  - Query cross-entropy via Mahalanobis mixture head; bound-proxy regularizers computed on support; complexity penalties c_d·d + c_r·r with larger multipliers when k≤3; L2 on τ_P deviation from 1; sparsity on m_y (e.g., l0 proxy via hard-concrete or simple entropy penalty on m distribution)
- Optimizer: AdamW lr=1e-3, wd=1e-4, 60k–100k episodes; cosine decay to 1e-4; gradient clipping 1.0; dropout 0.1 in H
- Safeguards: Woodbury inverses with jitter 1e-4; cap d ≤ 64 (or min(D,64)); cap r ≤ min(d,8); clip τ_P to [0.5, 2.0]

Inference core (inductive default)
- Build P, Σ_y, m_y, τ_P, (Ř, λ) from support stats; project all features; compute mixture-of-prototype Mahalanobis distances using Woodbury inverse; return logits = − distance − log π_yj

Transductive refinement (optional branch)
- Implement two options in P-space:
  - LabelSpreading (scikit-learn) with kNN affinity (k=10–20), alpha tuned by λ; seed labels via prototype posteriors on queries; run 3–5 iters (max_iter), gamma/alpha from H
  - ProtoLP (prototype-based label propagation): propagate soft labels from support-defined prototypes to queries using graph Laplacian; can approximate via LabelSpreading with support points included and weighted priors
- Gate: If Ř > θ (calibrated threshold), run transductive refinement; otherwise stay inductive

Risk calibration and conformal threshold
- Train Ř to regress held-out query CE using support-only proxies; fit isotonic regression g(Ř) on base-val episodes to calibrate monotonic mapping to expected loss or improvement
- Split-conformal: Use a calibration set of episodes (base-val and optionally unlabeled small target pool) to set θ so that probability of calling when improvement<0 is ≤ α (e.g., 0.1). Specifically, compute improvement Δ per episode of 3-iter LaplacianShot vs inductive; set θ to the α-quantile of g(Ř) among negatively improving episodes

Statistical protocol
- For each condition, 10,000 episodes; bootstrap 95% CI; paired episode alignment across methods for fair comparisons; Wilcoxon signed-rank test for per-episode accuracy differences
- Report runtime per episode (mean±std) excluding feature extraction; record transductive call-rate and win-rate


Experiment 1 — k-mismatch robustness on in-domain few-shot

Goal and hypotheses
- Goal: Demonstrate that per-episode geometry adaptation reduces sensitivity to test-time shot count and improves accuracy across ways/shots compared to static metrics and unconditional transduction
- H1: Higher accuracy across k ∈ {1,2,5,8,10}, N ∈ {5,10,15,20}
- H2: Flatter k-mismatch curve (smaller accuracy drop vs train k)
- H3: Latency close to inductive baselines due to low transductive call-rate

Data/backbones
- Datasets: miniImageNet and tieredImageNet
- Backbone: ResNet-12 trained once per dataset with bound-aware losses; freeze and cache features

Baselines
- ProtoNet (Euclidean), SimpleShot (CL2N), LDA/EST transforms (global linear), FeCAM (diag and full with shrinkage), LaplacianShot and protoLP (unconditional), Multi-proto ProtoNet (fixed m=2)
- BEMeGA ablations: P-only, Σ-only, m-only, no-gate (always inductive), unconditional transductive, shared-only covariances

Protocol
- Meta-train H on base classes with episodes N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}
- Evaluate on novel classes with N ∈ {5,10,15,20} × k ∈ {1,2,5,8,10}; q=15
- For each condition: 10k episodes; collect adapter decisions (d, r, m_y, τ_P, Ř)

Metrics
- Accuracy ±95% CI
- k-mismatch sensitivity: fit linear model accuracy vs |k−k_train| and compare slopes; compute area-under-k-curve per N
- Runtime per episode, transductive call-rate and win-rate

Analyses
- Accuracy vs baselines per (N,k)
- Scatter plots: d and r chosen vs k
- Correlate bound proxies (on support) with Ř and with actual gains from transduction (for episodes where it fires)

Success criteria
- Significant gains over L2/EST/FeCAM at k∈{1,2}; sustained gains at k≥5
- 20–40% reduction in mismatch slope vs ProtoNet
- Transductive call-rate ≤25% with >60% win-rate on called episodes

Example evaluation loop (sketch)
- Pseudocode, relies on helper functions defined later:

  for N in [5,10,15,20]:
      for k in [1,2,5,8,10]:
          episodes = sample_episodes(n_episodes=10000, N=N, k=k, q=15, split='novel')
          for ep in episodes:
              Zs, ys, Zq, yq = ep.support_feats, ep.support_labels, ep.query_feats, ep.query_labels
              S = compute_support_stats(Zs, ys, stats_cache)
              P, params = adapter_H(S)  # includes d, r, tau_P, cov params, m_y, risk_hat, lambda
              ZsP = (Zs @ P) * params.tau_P; ZqP = (Zq @ P) * params.tau_P
              protos = init_multi_protos(ZsP, ys, m_vec=params.m_vec)  # per-class
              covs = build_covariances(ZsP, ys, params)
              if params.risk_hat > theta:
                  logits = transductive_refine(ZsP, ys, ZqP, protos, covs, params.lambda)
              else:
                  logits = mahalanobis_mixture_logits(ZqP, protos, covs, params.pi)
              pred = logits.argmax(-1)
              log_episode_metrics(...)

Reliability enhancements used here
- Paired episode alignment across methods; identical episodes and seeds
- Confidence intervals via bootstrap; Wilcoxon signed-rank per episode for p-values
- Runtime measured with torch.cuda.Event to avoid Python timing noise
- Sanity check: verify that when forcing d=full and r=0, BEMeGA reduces to a spherical ProtoNet variant; expect similar numbers


Experiment 2 — Stress-test anisotropy and multimodality (controlled + real)

Goal and hypotheses
- Goal: Validate that anisotropic covariances and multi-prototype selection drive gains under anisotropy/multimodality
- H4: On synthetic Gaussian episodes with known structure, BEMeGA selects correct m_y and beats spherical metrics
- H5: On real images with induced multimodality, BEMeGA’s m>1 and low-rank covariances yield significant gains, especially at k∈{1,2}

A) Controlled synthetic episodes
- Generator (R^D with D=50):
  - Per class y, draw mode count M ∈ {1,2,3} with P(1)=0.6, P(2)=0.3, P(3)=0.1
  - Centers μ_{y,j} ~ N(0, σ_c^2 I), σ_c=3
  - Anisotropic covariance: Σ_{y,j} = Q diag(λ) Q^T with Q random orthonormal (Haar), λ_i ∝ i^{−p} with p ~ U[0.5,2.0], scaled to trace τ ~ U[0.5,2.0]
  - Draw support and query points per episode accordingly
- Training H: 100k episodes with N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}, q=20; disable image backbone and use Z directly; dictionary atoms sampled as random orthonormal sets and diag bins; keep identical H architecture
- Baselines: spherical ProtoNet (Euclidean), global LDA+ spherical, diagonal Mahalanobis with shared shrinkage, multi-proto fixed m=2 spherical, FeCAM-style diagonal with uniform shrinkage
- Metrics: accuracy ±95% CI; m_y selection accuracy (binary: M>1 vs predicted m>1); Spearman correlation between selected rank r and true spectrum decay p; chosen d vs oracle PCA dimension achieving 90% explained variance
- Expectation: BEMeGA strongly outperforms spherical baselines when M>1 or p small (heavy anisotropy); near-tied on unimodal spherical classes where it chooses m=1 and small r

Notes for reliability
- Use the same random seeds and class compositions across methods; record ground-truth M and p per episode; evaluate subsets by ground-truth structure
- Disable transductive gate (inductive only) to isolate geometry effects

B) Real multimodality: Dual-mode CIFAR-FS
- Construct dataset: For each image, with p=0.5 apply either a 90° rotation or strong color-jitter+solarize; keep original label; apply the same augmentation policy during backbone pretraining and evaluation sampling to ensure multimodality
- Backbone: ResNet-12 bound-aware pretraining on augmented base
- Protocol: Meta-train H on base; evaluate on novel with N∈{5,10} and k∈{1,2,5}; 10k episodes/condition
- Baselines: those in Experiment 1
- Metrics: accuracy ±95% CI; proportion of episodes with m>1; accuracy uplift conditioned on m>1 vs m=1; evaluate forced m=1 variant of BEMeGA to isolate multi-prototype effect
- Expectation: >3–5% absolute gain over spherical baselines at k∈{1,2}; BEMeGA chooses m>1 in >60% episodes under strong multimodality


Experiment 3 — Cross-domain robustness and calibrated transductive gating

Goal and hypotheses
- Goal: Show inductive-first efficiency and safe, calibrated transductive gains under domain shift
- H6: On miniImageNet→CUB, BEMeGA-inductive > static baselines
- H7: Gate triggers on hard episodes with positive win-rate and bounded call-rate
- H8: Split-conformal θ ensures non-negative expected improvement and better runtime-accuracy trade-off than unconditional transduction

Data/backbones
- Train backbone and H on miniImageNet base; evaluate on miniImageNet novel (in-domain) and CUB novel (cross-domain). Backbones: ResNet-12 and ViT-S/16

Gating and calibration setup
- Train Ř on base-val to predict held-out query CE using support-only proxies; fit scikit-learn IsotonicRegression to map Ř → calibrated risk g(Ř)
- Split-conformal: Use 5k episodes from base-val and 5k from a small unlabeled CUB pool (no labels used for fitting H). For each episode, compute Δ accuracy of 3-iter LaplacianShot vs inductive on pseudo-labels from base-val or by revealing query labels only for calibration. Set θ at α-quantile (α=0.1) of g(Ř) among episodes with Δ<0; this caps the false-call rate on harmful episodes

Protocol
- Evaluate on novel miniImageNet and CUB with N∈{5,10}, k∈{1,5}, q=15; 10k episodes/condition
- Methods: BEMeGA-inductive; BEMeGA+Gate (calibrated); unconditional LaplacianShot/protoLP in P-space; ProtoNet/FeCAM/LDA

Metrics and analyses
- Accuracy ±95% CI; call-rate; win-rate; expected improvement (gated − inductive); runtime per episode
- Calibration metrics for g(Ř): ECE (expected calibration error), Brier score, reliability plot (risk deciles vs observed error)
- Pareto plot of accuracy vs runtime; sweep θ to trace curve; highlight conformal θ operating point

Success criteria
- BEMeGA+Gate improves over inductive-only; within 10–20% of unconditional transduction’s gain at ≤30% runtime cost; expected improvement ≥ 0 by construction; ECE < 0.1


Key ablations (run once; reused across experiments)
- Remove bound-aware pretraining penalties: train a backbone with plain CE; redo Exp1 N=10, k={1,5} and Exp3 CUB N=5, k=1; expect drops in bound proxies and accuracy; larger k-mismatch
- Disable dictionary: allow free linear P; expect stability issues, worse calibration, higher variance of d and r, and slightly worse accuracy
- Force d=full, r=0: reduces to spherical ProtoNet in projected space; quantify effect at k∈{1,2}
- Force single prototype m=1: isolate gains due to multi-prototypes in Exp2 (A and B)
- No gate vs unconditional transduction vs calibrated gate: quantify runtime-accuracy tradeoffs in Exp3


Logging and diagnostics
- Per episode: chosen d, r, m_y, τ_P; Σ trace dispersion before/after; within/between trace ratio; norm variance; Ř; gate decision; transduction improvement if run; runtime breakdown
- Aggregate: histograms of d, r, m_y vs k; correlation of bound proxies with accuracy or with gains; calibration reliability diagrams
- Integrity checks: PSD of Σ_y; condition numbers; number of failed QR re-orthonormalizations (should be zero)


Reproducibility details
- Seeds: 5 seeds; report mean across seeds; within each seed, report CI over episodes; share seed values in logs
- Hardware: 1×A100 40GB or 3090 24GB; meta-training H ~6–8 GPU hours per dataset (frozen features); experiments run on frozen features for speed and determinism
- Episode counts: 10k per condition (50k for synthetic to get precise curves)
- Caching: save feature tensors and dictionary bank to disk; version them with checksum in run config
- Code release: pin library versions; export environment.yml; provide scripts to regenerate dictionary bank and to run each experiment


Minimal helper implementations (PyTorch-oriented sketches)

1) Bound-aware pretraining losses

Def norm variance penalty (batch features F: B×D):
  def norm_variance_penalty(F):
      sq_norms = (F.pow(2).sum(dim=1))
      return sq_norms.var(unbiased=False)

Def trace ratio proxy (within/between):
  def within_between_trace_ratio(F, y, eps=1e-6):
      classes = torch.unique(y)
      mu = F.mean(dim=0)
      tw = 0.0; tb = 0.0
      for c in classes:
          Fc = F[y==c]
          mc = Fc.mean(dim=0)
          tw += ((Fc - mc).T @ (Fc - mc)).trace()
          n_c = Fc.size(0)
          diff = (mc - mu).unsqueeze(1)
          tb += (n_c * (diff @ diff.T)).trace()
      return tw / (tb + eps)

Def trace-equality penalty (std of per-class covariance traces):
  from sklearn.covariance import LedoitWolf
  def trace_equality_penalty(F, y):
      classes = torch.unique(y)
      traces = []
      for c in classes:
          Fc = F[y==c].detach().cpu().numpy()
          if Fc.shape[0] >= 5:
              lw = LedoitWolf().fit(Fc)
              tr = np.trace(lw.covariance_)
          else:
              var = Fc.var(axis=0, ddof=1).sum()
              tr = var
          traces.append(tr)
      traces = torch.tensor(traces, dtype=F.dtype, device=F.device)
      return traces.std(unbiased=False)

2) Dictionary bank creation

  def build_dictionary(features, labels, top_k=64, rhos=(0.1,0.3,0.5)):
      # features: (N,D), labels: (N,)
      X = features - features.mean(0, keepdim=True)
      D = X.size(1)
      classes = torch.unique(labels)
      mus = []
      Sw = torch.zeros(D, D, device=X.device)
      for c in classes:
          Xc = X[labels==c]
          mc = Xc.mean(0)
          mus.append(mc)
          Xc0 = Xc - mc
          Sw += Xc0.T @ Xc0 / max(1, Xc0.size(0)-1)
      mus = torch.stack(mus)
      mu0 = mus - mus.mean(0, keepdim=True)
      Smu = mu0.T @ mu0 / max(1, mu0.size(0)-1)
      atoms = []
      # Σ_μ and Σ_s atoms
      for M in [Smu, Sw]:
          evals, evecs = torch.linalg.eigh(M)
          U = evecs[:, -top_k:]
          atoms.append(U)
      # A_rho atoms
      for rho in rhos:
          A = Smu - rho * Sw
          evals, evecs = torch.linalg.eigh(A)
          U = evecs[:, -top_k:]
          atoms.append(U)
      # Diagonal variance bins
      var_dims = X.pow(2).mean(0) - X.mean(0).pow(2)
      q = torch.quantile(var_dims, torch.linspace(0,1,9, device=X.device))
      diag_bins = []
      for i in range(8):
          mask = (var_dims>=q[i]) & (var_dims<=q[i+1])
          diag_bins.append(mask.nonzero(as_tuple=False).squeeze(1).cpu().tolist())
      return { 'subspace_atoms': atoms, 'diag_bins': diag_bins, 'mean_norm': float((features.pow(2).sum(1).sqrt()).mean().cpu()) }

3) Support statistics

  from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_samples
  from sklearn.neighbors import NearestNeighbors
  from sklearn.covariance import LedoitWolf

  def james_stein_trace_shrink(per_class_arrays):
      # Shrink per-class trace to pooled trace when sample is tiny
      traces = []
      D = per_class_arrays[0].shape[1]
      pooled = np.concatenate(per_class_arrays, axis=0)
      pooled_tr = pooled.var(axis=0, ddof=1).sum()
      for X in per_class_arrays:
          n = X.shape[0]
          raw_tr = X.var(axis=0, ddof=1).sum() if n>1 else pooled_tr
          # alpha_js in [0,1]: more shrinkage when n small
          alpha = min(1.0, max(0.0, (D-2)/max(1,n-1)))
          tr = (1-alpha)*raw_tr + alpha*pooled_tr
          traces.append(tr)
      return np.array(traces)

  def small_sample_silhouette(Z, y):
      # Normalize then silhouette with correction for small sample sizes
      Zc = torch.nn.functional.normalize(Z, dim=1)
      Zc_np = Zc.detach().cpu().numpy()
      y_np = y.detach().cpu().numpy()
      if len(np.unique(y_np))<2: return 0.0
      try:
          sil = silhouette_samples(Zc_np, y_np, metric='cosine')
          return float(np.nanmean(sil))
      except Exception:
          return 0.0

  def intra_class_nn_ratio(Z, y, k=1):
      Z_np = Z.detach().cpu().numpy()
      nbrs = NearestNeighbors(n_neighbors=k+1, algorithm='auto').fit(Z_np)
      _, idx = nbrs.kneighbors(Z_np)
      idx = idx[:,1:]  # drop self
      y_np = y.detach().cpu().numpy()
      hits = 0; total = Z_np.shape[0]*k
      for i in range(Z_np.shape[0]):
          hits += (y_np[idx[i]] == y_np[i]).sum()
      return hits/total

  def compute_support_stats(Z, y, stats_cache):
      classes = torch.unique(y)
      per_class = [Z[y==c] for c in classes]
      means = [X.mean(0) for X in per_class]
      means = torch.stack(means)
      pairwise = torch.cdist(means, means, p=2)
      norm_var = (Z.pow(2).sum(1)).var(unbiased=False)
      # traces: LedoitWolf if n>=5 else James–Stein to pooled
      traces = []
      js_tr = None
      all_np = [X.detach().cpu().numpy() for X in per_class]
      for X in per_class:
          Xnp = X.detach().cpu().numpy()
          if Xnp.shape[0] >= 5:
              cov = LedoitWolf().fit(Xnp).covariance_
              traces.append(np.trace(cov))
          else:
              if js_tr is None:
                  js_tr = james_stein_trace_shrink(all_np)
              traces = js_tr; break
      traces_t = torch.tensor(traces, dtype=Z.dtype, device=Z.device)
      sil = small_sample_silhouette(Z, y)
      nnr = intra_class_nn_ratio(Z, y)
      # Within/between
      tw_tb = within_between_trace_ratio(Z, y)
      # Norm-scale vs base mean norm
      mean_norm = float(torch.norm(Z, dim=1).mean())
      base_norm = stats_cache.get('mean_norm', mean_norm)
      norm_scale_ratio = mean_norm / (base_norm + 1e-6)
      # Cross-class trace dispersion
      trace_disp = traces_t.std(unbiased=False)
      return {
          'means': means, 'pairwise': pairwise, 'norm_var': norm_var,
          'traces': traces_t, 'silhouette': sil, 'intra_nn_ratio': nnr,
          'tw_tb_ratio': tw_tb, 'norm_scale_ratio': norm_scale_ratio,
          'N': int(len(classes)), 'k': int(per_class[0].size(0))
      }

4) Build projection P from atoms with Stiefel penalty

  def build_P(weights, atoms, d):
      # atoms: list of D×k_i; weights: len(atoms) nonnegative; ensure softmax outside caller
      D = atoms[0].size(0)
      U = torch.zeros(D, sum(a.size(1) for a in atoms), device=atoms[0].device, dtype=atoms[0].dtype)
      col = 0
      for w, A in zip(weights, atoms):
          U[:, col:col+A.size(1)] = w * A
          col += A.size(1)
      # Re-orthonormalize
      Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(U, mode='reduced')
      d_sel = min(d, Q.size(1))
      P = Q[:, :d_sel]
      return P

  def stiefel_penalty(P):
      I = torch.eye(P.size(1), device=P.device, dtype=P.dtype)
      return torch.norm(P.T @ P - I, p='fro')**2

5) Covariance factory and Woodbury inverse

  def build_covariances(ZsP, ys, params):
      # params provides sigma2 per class or shared, low-rank basis B selection, shrinkages, blend β
      classes = torch.unique(ys)
      covs = {}
      for c in classes:
          Xc = ZsP[ys==c]
          d = ZsP.size(1)
          sigma2 = torch.clamp(params.sigma2[c], 1e-5, 10.0)
          # Build B from dictionary-provided basis vectors projected into P-space or top PCs of Xc (fallback)
          if params.use_dict_B:
              Bc = params.B_dict[c]  # shape d×r
          else:
              U, S, Vh = torch.pca_lowrank(Xc, q=min(8, d))
              r = min(params.rank_r, U.size(1))
              Bc = U[:, :r] * S[:r].sqrt()
          # Shared↔class-specific blend: interpolate towards shared B across classes when k tiny
          if params.beta < 1.0:
              B_shared = torch.stack([params.B_dict[c] for c in classes]).mean(0)
              Bc = params.beta * Bc + (1-params.beta) * B_shared
          covs[int(c.item())] = { 'sigma2': sigma2, 'B': Bc }
      return covs

  def mahalanobis_mixture_logits(ZqP, protos, covs, priors):
      # protos: dict class->list of centers in P-space; covs dict class->(sigma2, B)
      logits = []
      for c, C_list in protos.items():
          sigma2 = covs[c]['sigma2']
          B = covs[c]['B']  # d×r
          # Precompute Woodbury components
          d = ZqP.size(1)
          if B.numel() == 0:
              # purely spherical
              inv = (1.0/sigma2)
              # distance per center
              dist_min = None
              for mu in C_list:
                  diff = ZqP - mu.unsqueeze(0)
                  dval = (diff.pow(2).sum(1)) * inv
                  dist_min = dval if dist_min is None else torch.minimum(dist_min, dval)
          else:
              # Woodbury: (σ^2 I + B B^T)^{-1} x = σ^{-2} x − σ^{-2} B (I + σ^{-2} B^T B)^{-1} B^T σ^{-2} x
              BtB = (B.T @ B) / (sigma2)
              M = torch.linalg.inv(torch.eye(BtB.size(0), device=B.device, dtype=B.dtype) + BtB)
              dist_min = None
              for mu in C_list:
                  diff = ZqP - mu.unsqueeze(0)  # n×d
                  t1 = diff / sigma2
                  t2 = (t1 @ B) @ M @ (B.T / sigma2)
                  Qx = t1 - t2
                  dval = (diff * Qx).sum(1)
                  dist_min = dval if dist_min is None else torch.minimum(dist_min, dval)
          logit_c = -dist_min + math.log(priors.get(c, 1.0/len(protos)))
          logits.append(logit_c.unsqueeze(1))
      return torch.cat(logits, dim=1)

6) Multi-prototype initialization (spherical k-means on normalized P-space)

  from sklearn.cluster import KMeans

  def init_multi_protos(ZsP, ys, m_vec):
      protos = {}
      classes = torch.unique(ys)
      ZsPn = torch.nn.functional.normalize(ZsP, dim=1)
      for c in classes:
          Xc = ZsPn[ys==c].detach().cpu().numpy()
          m = int(m_vec[int(c.item())])
          if Xc.shape[0] < m:
              # fallback: use unique points or pad with means
              centers = [Xc.mean(0) for _ in range(m)]
          else:
              km = KMeans(n_clusters=m, n_init='auto', max_iter=50, random_state=0)
              km.fit(Xc)
              centers = km.cluster_centers_
          centers = torch.tensor(centers, device=ZsP.device, dtype=ZsP.dtype)
          # Un-normalize by projecting back to non-normalized space via average norm
          norms = ZsP[ys==c].norm(dim=1).mean()
          centers = centers * norms
          protos[int(c.item())] = [centers[i] for i in range(centers.size(0))]
      return protos

7) Transductive refinement via LabelSpreading (approx LaplacianShot)

  from sklearn.semi_supervised import LabelSpreading
  from sklearn.neighbors import kneighbors_graph

  def transductive_refine(ZsP, ys, ZqP, protos, covs, lam):
      # Build pseudo-labels for query via inductive logits as initialization
      priors = {c: 1.0/len(protos) for c in protos}
      logits0 = mahalanobis_mixture_logits(ZqP, protos, covs, priors)
      y0 = logits0.softmax(dim=1).detach().cpu().numpy()
      # Build labeled set: use support centroids as labeled nodes
      classes = sorted(list(protos.keys()))
      Cmat = torch.stack([torch.stack(protos[c]).mean(0) for c in classes])  # one centroid per class for seeding
      X_all = torch.cat([Cmat, ZqP], dim=0).detach().cpu().numpy()
      y_seed = np.array([i for i,_ in enumerate(classes)])
      y_lab = np.full((X_all.shape[0],), -1, dtype=int)
      y_lab[:len(classes)] = y_seed
      # Affinity graph
      G = kneighbors_graph(X_all, n_neighbors=15, mode='connectivity', include_self=True)
      ls = LabelSpreading(kernel='knn', n_neighbors=15, max_iter=5, alpha=float(lam))
      ls.fit(X_all, y_lab)
      proba = ls.label_distributions_[len(classes):]
      return torch.tensor(proba, device=ZqP.device, dtype=ZqP.dtype)

8) Risk calibration with isotonic regression and split-conformal threshold

  from sklearn.isotonic import IsotonicRegression

  def fit_isotonic(risk_hat_list, target_list):
      # risk_hat_list: raw Ř; target_list: observed per-episode loss or improvement
      ir = IsotonicRegression(out_of_bounds='clip')
      ir.fit(np.array(risk_hat_list), np.array(target_list))
      return ir

  def conformal_threshold(ir, risk_hat_list, improvement_list, alpha=0.1):
      g = ir.predict(np.array(risk_hat_list))
      neg = np.array(improvement_list) < 0
      theta = np.quantile(g[neg], 1-alpha) if neg.any() else g.max()+1e-6
      return theta

9) Training loop sketch for H

  for it, batch in enumerate(episode_loader):
      # batch contains multiple episodes; each episode has (Zs, ys, Zq, yq)
      loss_total = 0.0
      for ep in batch:
          Zs, ys, Zq, yq = ep
          S = compute_support_stats(Zs, ys, stats_cache)
          P, params = adapter_H(S)
          ZsP = (Zs @ P) * params.tau_P; ZqP = (Zq @ P) * params.tau_P
          protos = init_multi_protos(ZsP, ys, params.m_vec)
          covs = build_covariances(ZsP, ys, params)
          logits = mahalanobis_mixture_logits(ZqP, protos, covs, params.pi)
          ce = torch.nn.functional.cross_entropy(logits, yq)
          # bound proxies on support
          nv = norm_variance_penalty(ZsP)
          fr = within_between_trace_ratio(ZsP, ys)
          te = torch.tensor(S['traces']).std() if isinstance(S['traces'], torch.Tensor) else torch.tensor(S['traces'])
          comp = params.c_d * params.d + params.c_r * params.r
          pen = stiefel_penalty(P) + (params.tau_P-1.0).pow(2)
          loss = ce + λ_nv*nv + λ_fr*fr + λ_te*te + comp + 1e-3*pen
          loss_total = loss_total + loss
      optimizer.zero_grad()
      loss_total.backward()
      torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(adapter_H.parameters(), 1.0)
      optimizer.step()


What each experiment uniquely demonstrates (integration and non-overlap)
- Experiment 1 focuses on k-mismatch and efficiency with the full adapter including P, Σ, m, and the gate. It evaluates overall robustness across in-domain ways/shots.
- Experiment 2 isolates anisotropy and multimodality factors: (A) shows selection fidelity and behavior under known ground-truth structure; (B) shows real-world relevance with induced dual modes. It disables gating to avoid conflating with transduction and concentrates on Σ and m.
- Experiment 3 isolates the calibrated risk gate and cross-domain performance. It maintains BEMeGA’s inductive geometry and tests the gating mechanism’s calibration and Pareto efficiency under domain shift.


Practical tips and additional reliability guards
- PSD-by-design: clamp eigenvalues in all inversions; add 1e-4 jitter to Σ_y
- Numerical stability: use double precision in QR and eigh when D>512; cast back to float32
- Episode normalization: z-score features per episode optionally before stats; record both raw and normalized proxies
- Early-stop meta-training of H using base-val accuracy and proxy trends; patience 10k episodes
- Hyperparameter tuning minimal: keep defaults; only adjust λs lightly on base-val to avoid overfitting
- Code modularity: Adapter H exposes a single forward(S) returning structured params; all utilities pure functions; enable TorchScript where feasible

End of specification.
experimental_design.experiment_code:
# BEMeGA: Bound‑conditioned Episodic Metric and Geometry Adapter for Robust Few‑shot Prototypes
# Minimal, reproducible PyTorch-centric experiment code with synthetic episodes
# - Implements Experiments 1–3 in a fast-to-run setting (synthetic data)
# - Includes plotting to .pdf files and clear stdout prints
# - Provides a quick test() that executes small runs to verify functionality
#
# NOTE: This code focuses on the episodic adapter/inference core and robustness demonstrations
# with synthetic data that mimics anisotropy, multimodality, and domain shift.
# It is structured so it can be extended to real feature caches if desired.

import math
import os
import random
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, List, Tuple

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# ------------------------------
# Utilities: Reproducibility
# ------------------------------

def set_seed(seed: int = 42):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(seed)
    torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = False
    torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True


# ------------------------------
# Dictionary bank (subspace + diag bins) and builders
# ------------------------------

@dataclass
class DictionaryBank:
    subspace_atoms: List[torch.Tensor]  # list of D x k_i atoms
    diag_bins: List[List[int]]          # dimension index bins
    mean_norm: float                    # reference feature norm (for proxies)


def build_random_dictionary(D: int, top_k: int = 32, n_atoms: int = 5, n_bins: int = 8, device: str = "cpu") -> DictionaryBank:
    """
    Build a random dictionary bank with:
    - n_atoms random orthonormal subspaces of width top_k (QR-orthonormalized)
    - n_bins diagonal bins splitting dimensions evenly
    """
    atoms = []
    for _ in range(n_atoms):
        A = torch.randn(D, top_k, device=device)
        Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(A, mode="reduced")
        atoms.append(Q)
    # diag bins: split dims into equal bins
    all_dims = list(range(D))
    bins = []
    stride = max(1, D // n_bins)
    for i in range(n_bins):
        start = i * stride
        end = D if i == n_bins - 1 else (i + 1) * stride
        bins.append(all_dims[start:end])
    return DictionaryBank(subspace_atoms=atoms, diag_bins=bins, mean_norm=1.0)


# ------------------------------
# Support statistics and bound proxies
# ------------------------------

def within_between_trace_ratio(Z: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, eps: float = 1e-6) -> torch.Tensor:
    classes = torch.unique(y)
    mu_all = Z.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
    tw = torch.tensor(0.0, device=Z.device, dtype=Z.dtype)
    tb = torch.tensor(0.0, device=Z.device, dtype=Z.dtype)
    for c in classes:
        Xc = Z[y == c]
        mc = Xc.mean(dim=0, keepdim=True)
        Xc0 = Xc - mc
        # trace of within scatter
        tw = tw + torch.sum(Xc0.pow(2))
        # between scatter weighted by size
        diff = (mc - mu_all)
        tb = tb + Xc.shape[0] * torch.sum(diff.pow(2))
    return tw / (tb + eps)


def small_sample_silhouette_torch(Z: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor) -> float:
    """Fast silhouette proxy with cosine distance on normalized features.
    Returns mean silhouette over all points. Robust for very small k.
    """
    if torch.unique(y).numel() < 2:
        return 0.0
    X = F.normalize(Z, dim=1)
    # cosine distance matrix
    S = X @ X.t()
    Dcos = 1.0 - S
    y_np = y.detach().cpu().numpy()
    n = Z.size(0)
    a = torch.zeros(n, device=Z.device, dtype=Z.dtype)
    b = torch.full((n,), float("inf"), device=Z.device, dtype=Z.dtype)
    for idx in range(n):
        c = y_np[idx]
        mask_same = torch.tensor(y_np == c, device=Z.device)
        mask_diff = torch.tensor(y_np != c, device=Z.device)
        # exclude self
        same_idx = torch.where(mask_same)[0]
        if same_idx.numel() > 1:
            a[idx] = Dcos[idx, mask_same].sum() / (same_idx.numel() - 1)
        # nearest other cluster mean distance
        unique_classes = np.unique(y_np[mask_diff.detach().cpu().numpy()])
        if unique_classes.size == 0:
            b[idx] = 0.0
        else:
            bmin = float("inf")
            for oc in unique_classes:
                mask_oc = torch.tensor(y_np == oc, device=Z.device)
                mean_dist = Dcos[idx, mask_oc].mean()
                bmin = min(bmin, float(mean_dist.item()))
            b[idx] = bmin
    s = (b - a) / (torch.maximum(a, b) + 1e-6)
    return float(torch.nan_to_num(s, nan=0.0).mean().item())


def intra_class_nn_ratio_torch(Z: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, k: int = 1) -> float:
    """Ratio of nearest neighbors that share the same label (support-only proxy)."""
    X = Z
    D = torch.cdist(X, X)
    # Exclude self by setting diagonal to big number
    D = D + torch.eye(D.size(0), device=D.device) * 1e6
    idx = torch.topk(D, k=k, largest=False).indices  # n x k
    y_rep = y.view(-1, 1).repeat(1, k)
    hits = (y_rep == y[idx]).sum().item()
    total = X.size(0) * k
    return hits / max(1, total)


def james_stein_trace_shrink_torch(per_class: List[torch.Tensor]) -> torch.Tensor:
    # Shrink per-class trace to pooled trace when sample is tiny
    D = per_class[0].size(1)
    pooled = torch.cat(per_class, dim=0)
    pooled_tr = pooled.var(dim=0, unbiased=True).sum()
    traces = []
    for X in per_class:
        n = X.size(0)
        if n > 1:
            raw_tr = X.var(dim=0, unbiased=True).sum()
        else:
            raw_tr = pooled_tr
        alpha = min(1.0, max(0.0, (D - 2) / max(1, n - 1)))
        tr = (1 - alpha) * raw_tr + alpha * pooled_tr
        traces.append(tr)
    return torch.stack(traces)


def compute_support_stats(Z: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, mean_norm_ref: float = 1.0) -> Dict:
    classes = torch.unique(y)
    per_class = [Z[y == c] for c in classes]
    means = torch.stack([X.mean(0) for X in per_class], dim=0)
    pairwise = torch.cdist(means, means)
    norm_var = (Z.pow(2).sum(1)).var(unbiased=False)
    # traces with JS shrinkage when n<5
    traces = []
    small = any([X.size(0) < 5 for X in per_class])
    if small:
        traces_t = james_stein_trace_shrink_torch(per_class)
    else:
        # unbiased variance traces
        traces_t = torch.stack([X.var(dim=0, unbiased=True).sum() for X in per_class])
    sil = small_sample_silhouette_torch(Z, y)
    nnr = intra_class_nn_ratio_torch(Z, y, k=1)
    tw_tb = within_between_trace_ratio(Z, y)
    mean_norm = torch.norm(Z, dim=1).mean().item()
    norm_scale_ratio = mean_norm / (mean_norm_ref + 1e-9)
    trace_disp = traces_t.std(unbiased=False)
    return {
        "means": means,
        "pairwise": pairwise,
        "norm_var": norm_var,
        "traces": traces_t,
        "silhouette": sil,
        "intra_nn_ratio": nnr,
        "tw_tb_ratio": tw_tb,
        "norm_scale_ratio": norm_scale_ratio,
        "trace_dispersion": trace_disp,
        "N": int(len(classes)),
        "k": int(per_class[0].size(0)) if len(per_class) > 0 else 0,
    }


# ------------------------------
# Projection and penalties
# ------------------------------

def build_P(weights: torch.Tensor, atoms: List[torch.Tensor], d: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    # weights: A (num_atoms,) softmaxed; atoms dims: list of D x k
    D = atoms[0].size(0)
    Ucols = sum([A.size(1) for A in atoms])
    U = torch.zeros(D, Ucols, device=atoms[0].device, dtype=atoms[0].dtype)
    col = 0
    for w, A in zip(weights, atoms):
        k_i = A.size(1)
        U[:, col:col + k_i] = w * A
        col += k_i
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(U, mode="reduced")
    d_sel = min(d, Q.size(1))
    return Q[:, :d_sel]


def stiefel_penalty(P: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    I = torch.eye(P.size(1), device=P.device, dtype=P.dtype)
    return torch.norm(P.T @ P - I, p="fro") ** 2


# ------------------------------
# Covariance factory + Woodbury Mahalanobis mixture
# ------------------------------

@dataclass
class CovParams:
    sigma2: Dict[int, torch.Tensor]
    B_dict: Dict[int, torch.Tensor]  # class -> d x r (can be empty tensor)


def mahalanobis_mixture_logits(Zq: torch.Tensor, protos: Dict[int, List[torch.Tensor]], covs: CovParams, priors: Dict[int, float]) -> torch.Tensor:
    classes = sorted(list(protos.keys()))
    C = len(classes)
    logits_per_c = []
    for c in classes:
        centers = protos[c]
        sigma2 = covs.sigma2[c]
        B = covs.B_dict[c]
        if B.numel() == 0:
            inv = 1.0 / sigma2
            dist_min = None
            for mu in centers:
                diff = Zq - mu.unsqueeze(0)
                dval = diff.pow(2).sum(1) * inv
                dist_min = dval if dist_min is None else torch.minimum(dist_min, dval)
        else:
            BtB = (B.T @ B) / sigma2
            M = torch.linalg.inv(torch.eye(BtB.size(0), device=B.device, dtype=B.dtype) + BtB)
            dist_min = None
            for mu in centers:
                diff = Zq - mu.unsqueeze(0)
                t1 = diff / sigma2
                t2 = (t1 @ B) @ M @ (B.T / sigma2)
                Qx = t1 - t2
                dval = (diff * Qx).sum(1)
                dist_min = dval if dist_min is None else torch.minimum(dist_min, dval)
        log_prior = math.log(priors.get(c, 1.0 / C))
        logits_per_c.append((-dist_min + log_prior).unsqueeze(1))
    return torch.cat(logits_per_c, dim=1)


# ------------------------------
# Simple spherical k-means for prototype multiplicity
# ------------------------------

def spherical_kmeans_torch(X: torch.Tensor, m: int, iters: int = 5) -> torch.Tensor:
    Xn = F.normalize(X, dim=1)
    n, d = Xn.shape
    if n < m:
        # pad with repeated mean
        mean = Xn.mean(0, keepdim=True)
        return mean.repeat(m, 1)
    # init with farthest-point heuristic
    centers = [Xn[torch.randint(0, n, (1,)).item()]]
    for _ in range(1, m):
        sims = torch.stack([Xn @ c for c in centers], dim=1)  # n x cur_m
        dists = 1 - sims.max(dim=1).values
        idx = torch.argmax(dists)
        centers.append(Xn[idx])
    C = torch.stack(centers, dim=0)
    for _ in range(iters):
        sims = Xn @ C.T  # n x m
        assign = sims.argmax(dim=1)
        newC = []
        for j in range(m):
            mask = (assign == j)
            if mask.any():
                cj = Xn[mask].mean(0)
                cj = F.normalize(cj, dim=0)
            else:
                cj = C[j]
            newC.append(cj)
        C = torch.stack(newC, dim=0)
    # rescale back to the avg norm of X
    avg_norm = X.norm(dim=1).mean().item()
    return C * avg_norm


# ------------------------------
# Transductive refinement (simple label propagation in P-space)
# ------------------------------

def simple_label_propagation(Zs: torch.Tensor, ys: torch.Tensor, Zq: torch.Tensor, logits_init: torch.Tensor, alpha: float = 0.5, k: int = 10, iters: int = 3) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Graph label propagation on query points with support centroids as anchors.
    - Build graph between queries and support centroids
    - Y0 initialized from logits_init softmax
    - Update Y <- alpha * S Y + (1-alpha) * Y0, where S is normalized affinity
    """
    with torch.no_grad():
        C = logits_init.size(1)
        # Support anchors: class means
        classes = torch.unique(ys)
        anchors = []
        for c in classes:
            anchors.append(Zs[ys == c].mean(0))
        A = torch.stack(anchors, dim=0)  # C x d
        # Build affinity for queries among themselves + to anchors (C nodes)
        Z_all = torch.cat([A, Zq], dim=0)
        # cosine affinity on normalized space
        ZN = F.normalize(Z_all, dim=1)
        sim = ZN @ ZN.t()
        # keep top-k neighbors per node
        topk = min(k, sim.size(0) - 1)
        mask = torch.zeros_like(sim)
        idx = torch.topk(sim, k=topk + 1, dim=1).indices  # include self
        rows = torch.arange(sim.size(0), device=sim.device).unsqueeze(1).repeat(1, topk + 1)
        mask[rows, idx] = 1.0
        mask = mask * (1 - torch.eye(sim.size(0), device=sim.device))  # drop self
        W = sim * mask
        W = F.relu(W)
        D = W.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True) + 1e-6
        S = W / D
        # Initialize labels: anchors are one-hot, queries from softmax of logits_init
        Y = torch.zeros(Z_all.size(0), C, device=Zq.device, dtype=Zq.dtype)
        Y[:C, :] = torch.eye(C, device=Zq.device, dtype=Zq.dtype)
        Y0q = F.softmax(logits_init, dim=1)
        Y[C:, :] = Y0q
        # Propagate only queries (anchors fixed via mixing with Y0)
        for _ in range(iters):
            Y = alpha * (S @ Y) + (1 - alpha) * Y
            # re-clamp anchors to be near one-hot
            Y[:C, :] = (1 - alpha) * torch.eye(C, device=Zq.device, dtype=Zq.dtype) + alpha * Y[:C, :]
        return Y[C:, :]


# ------------------------------
# BEMeGA Adapter (support-only, heuristic meta-parameters for demo)
# ------------------------------

@dataclass
class AdapterOutputs:
    P: torch.Tensor
    tau_P: float
    d: int
    r: int
    m_vec: Dict[int, int]
    cov_params: CovParams
    priors: Dict[int, float]
    risk_hat: float
    lam: float


class BEMeGAAdapter(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, D: int, dict_bank: DictionaryBank, max_d: int = 32, max_r: int = 6, device: str = "cpu"):
        super().__init__()
        self.D = D
        self.db = dict_bank
        self.max_d = min(max_d, D)
        self.max_r = min(max_r, self.max_d)
        self.device = device
        # Simple linear mapping from proxy vector to atom weights and scalar params
        proxy_dim = 6  # [norm_var, tw_tb, trace_disp, silhouette, nnr, norm_scale]
        self.atom_w = nn.Linear(proxy_dim, len(self.db.subspace_atoms))
        self.scale_head = nn.Linear(proxy_dim, 3)  # outputs for tau_P, d_gate, r_gate
        self.risk_head = nn.Linear(proxy_dim, 2)  # risk_hat, lambda
        # Initialize to small values
        for m in [self.atom_w, self.scale_head, self.risk_head]:
            nn.init.zeros_(m.weight)
            nn.init.zeros_(m.bias)

    def forward(self, stats: Dict) -> AdapterOutputs:
        # Build proxy vector
        with torch.no_grad():
            pvec = torch.tensor([
                float(stats["norm_var"]) if torch.is_tensor(stats["norm_var"]) else float(stats["norm_var"]),
                float(stats["tw_tb_ratio"]) if torch.is_tensor(stats["tw_tb_ratio"]) else float(stats["tw_tb_ratio"]),
                float(stats["trace_dispersion"]) if torch.is_tensor(stats["trace_dispersion"]) else float(stats["trace_dispersion"]),
                float(stats["silhouette"]),
                float(stats["intra_nn_ratio"]),
                float(stats["norm_scale_ratio"])],
                device=self.device, dtype=torch.float32)
        # Atom weights via softmax
        w = F.softmax(self.atom_w(pvec), dim=0)
        # Choose d, r, tau
        s_out = self.scale_head(pvec)
        tau_P = float(torch.sigmoid(s_out[0]) * 1.0 + 0.5)  # in [0.5, 1.5]
        # heuristic gates: encourage lower d, r when k small or anisotropy low
        k = stats["k"]
        N = stats["N"]
        base_d = 8 if k <= 2 else 12
        add_d = 8 if (float(stats["tw_tb_ratio"]) < 1.0) else 4
        d = int(min(self.max_d, max(4, base_d + add_d)))
        # rank r depends on anisotropy (trace_disp) and silhouette
        td = float(stats["trace_dispersion"]) if torch.is_tensor(stats["trace_dispersion"]) else float(stats["trace_dispersion"])
        sil = float(stats["silhouette"])
        r = 0 if (k == 1 and td < 1e-3) else int(max(0, min(self.max_r, 1 + int(5.0 * (td > 0) * (1 - sil)))))
        # Projection P
        P = build_P(w, self.db.subspace_atoms, d)
        # Covariance params per class: shared sigma2 scaled by traces; B from top-d columns of P scaled
        classes = list(range(N))
        sigma2 = {}
        B_dict = {}
        traces = stats["traces"]
        if torch.is_tensor(traces):
            traces_t = traces.detach()
        else:
            traces_t = torch.tensor(traces, device=self.device)
        tr_mean = float(traces_t.mean().item() if traces_t.numel() > 0 else 1.0)
        for ci, c in enumerate(classes):
            sigma2[c] = torch.tensor(max(1e-3, tr_mean / max(1, d)), device=self.device)
            if r <= 0:
                B_dict[c] = torch.zeros(d, 0, device=self.device)
            else:
                # choose first r columns of P scaled
                Bc = P[:, :r] * math.sqrt(max(1e-6, tr_mean / max(1, d)))
                B_dict[c] = Bc.clone()
        cov = CovParams(sigma2=sigma2, B_dict=B_dict)
        # Prototype multiplicity per class
        m_vec = {}
        nnr = float(stats["intra_nn_ratio"])  # higher is more single-mode
        for c in classes:
            if k == 1:
                m = 1
            else:
                # low silhouette and low nnr => multi-prototype
                if sil < 0.1 and nnr < 0.6:
                    m = 3
                elif sil < 0.2 and nnr < 0.7:
                    m = 2
                else:
                    m = 1
            m_vec[c] = m
        # Priors uniform
        priors = {c: 1.0 / N for c in classes}
        # Risk and lambda
        risk_lambda = self.risk_head(pvec)
        risk_hat = float(torch.sigmoid(risk_lambda[0]).item())  # ~[0,1]
        lam = float(torch.sigmoid(risk_lambda[1]).item())  # ~[0,1]
        return AdapterOutputs(P=P, tau_P=tau_P, d=d, r=r, m_vec=m_vec, cov_params=cov, priors=priors, risk_hat=risk_hat, lam=lam)


# ------------------------------
# Synthetic episode generator (anisotropy + multimodality)
# ------------------------------

@dataclass
class Episode:
    Zs: torch.Tensor
    ys: torch.Tensor
    Zq: torch.Tensor
    yq: torch.Tensor
    true_modes: Dict[int, int]   # ground-truth mode counts per class
    anisotropy_p: Dict[int, float]  # spectrum decay p per class (low p => heavy anisotropy)


def random_orthonormal(D: int, device: str = "cpu") -> torch.Tensor:
    A = torch.randn(D, D, device=device)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(A)
